sections.title: Lesson 1: What does it all mean?
sections.definition: Banking vocabulary and non-defining relative clauses.
sections.explanation: Understanding bank statements and common banking terms like account number, balance, transaction, withdrawal, deposit.
sections.notes: Non-defining relative clauses add extra information and are separated by commas.

examples.problem: bank statement
examples.solution: paper that tells you about what happened to your bank account in the past month

examples.problem: account number
examples.solution: the number that is used to identify your account

examples.problem: balance
examples.solution: the total amount of money in your account

sections.title: Banking Definitions
sections.definition: Definitions of common banking terms.
sections.explanation: Bank card, ATM, PIN, cheque, credit card are essential banking tools.
sections.notes: PIN is a secret number used with a bank card to prevent unauthorized use.

examples.problem: bank card =
examples.solution: a piece of plastic you can use for things or get money in the street

examples.problem: ATM =
examples.solution: somewhere you can get money using your bank card

examples.problem: PIN =
examples.solution: a secret number you can use with your bank card to stop others using it

sections.title: Grammar: Non-defining Relative Clauses
sections.definition: Non-defining relative clauses add extra information about a noun.
sections.explanation: They are separated by commas and use 'which' or 'who'. The sentence makes sense without them.
rules: Use commas to separate non-defining relative clauses.
Use 'which' for things and 'who' for people.
Do not use 'that' in non-defining relative clauses.
sections.notes: Your account number, which is an eight-digit number, appears on the top right.

examples.problem: Your bank statement is very important.
examples.solution: Your bank statement, which shows all your transactions, is very important.

examples.problem: Aziz is very helpful.
examples.solution: Aziz, who works at the bank, is very helpful.

sections.title: Banking Paragraph Completion
sections.definition: Using banking vocabulary to complete a paragraph.
sections.explanation: Common banking terms include: account, bank card, ATM, withdrawal, bank statement, credit card.
sections.notes: Keep track of spending to avoid overspending.

examples.problem: I opened a _______ last month.
Now I've got a _______ which I can use to get money.
I made a _______ of 12,000 Iraqi dinars.

sections.title: Lesson 2: Types of Bank Accounts
sections.definition: Different bank accounts serve different purposes.
sections.explanation: Savings accounts: for saving money. Current accounts: for daily transactions. Student accounts: for students. Each has different features.
sections.notes: Some accounts require a minimum balance; others offer overdraft facilities.

examples.problem: Which account is best if you want to save money to buy something expensive?
Which account can you get if you are 16?
Which account requires a minimum balance?

sections.title: Grammar: Passive Forms
sections.definition: The passive voice is used when the doer of the action is unknown or unimportant.
sections.explanation: Passive form: to be + past participle. The tense changes in the verb 'to be'.
rules: Use passive when the subject is less important than the action.
Use 'by' to mention the doer of the action.
Passive is often used to describe processes.
sections.notes: Example: The money was deposited last week by my uncle.

examples.problem: Somebody stole my wallet last week.
examples.solution: My wallet was stolen last week.

examples.problem: Somebody robbed the bank yesterday.
The cheque (sign) last week.
My ATM card (steal) yesterday.

sections.title: Lesson 3: Meet a Banker
sections.definition: Understanding a banker's daily duties and skills.
sections.explanation: Bankers assess business ideas, make loans, work with individuals and large companies. They need financial knowledge and communication skills.
sections.notes: Bankers use deposited money to make loans to others.

examples.problem: What does the banker like most about his job?
examples.solution: Helping people and the variety of work.

examples.problem: How does the bank use the money that people deposit?
examples.solution: They lend it to others for loans.

examples.problem: Why does a banker have to communicate well?
examples.solution: To explain financial products and understand clients' needs.

sections.title: Grammar: Passive vs Active Voice
sections.definition: Choosing between active and passive voice based on context.
sections.explanation: Active focuses on the doer; passive focuses on the action or recipient.
sections.notes: Use passive when the action is more important than who did it.

examples.problem: The money in a bank account (use) in many ways.
The machine (repair) at the moment.
Bankers usually (like) helping their customers.

sections.title: Grammar: Present and Past Deductions (Modals)
sections.definition: Using modal verbs to make deductions about present and past situations.
sections.explanation: Must: sure. May/Might: not sure. Can't: sure something didn't happen.
rules: Past deduction: must/may/might/can't + have + past participle
sections.notes: I can't find my wallet. I must have left it in the car.

examples.problem: I left some pizza in the fridge, but it's not there now. Saeed _______ (eat) it.
I'm not sure, but Razan _______ (be) at home already.
You _______ (finish) the test already. That's impossible!

sections.title: Grammar: Types of Conditional Sentences
sections.definition: Four types of conditionals: zero, first, second, and third.
sections.explanation: Zero: always true. First: real future possibility. Second: hypothetical present/future. Third: hypothetical past (impossible to change).
rules: Third: if + past perfect, would have + past participle
sections.notes: Third conditional expresses regret about past actions that cannot be changed.

examples.problem: If Sami hadn't forgotten his keys last night, he wouldn't have had to sleep in the car.
examples.solution: Third conditional - Sami forgot his keys, so he slept in the car.

examples.problem: If you studied harder, you would pass the exam easily.
examples.solution: Second conditional - You don't study hard, so you won't pass easily.

examples.problem: If people drive for too long, they get tired.
examples.solution: Zero conditional - People sometimes drive too long and get tired.

sections.title: Lesson 5: Travelling Abroad - Currency and Exchange
sections.definition: Vocabulary for international travel and currency exchange.
sections.explanation: Terms include: exchange rate, local currency, internationally accepted cards, prepaid debit cards, souvenirs.
sections.notes: Prepaid debit cards are safe because you can freeze them online if lost.

examples.problem: Hazem thinks his suitcase is too heavy to carry on the _______
He has a debit card that is accepted _______
He says a prepaid debit card is safe because you can _______ it online.

sections.title: Conditional Chain Exercises
sections.definition: Creating chains of conditional sentences.
sections.explanation: Each sentence's result becomes the condition for the next sentence.
sections.notes: First conditional chain: If I go to town, I'll spend money. If I spend money, I'll need more. etc.

examples.problem: If I go to town, I'll spend a lot of money.
examples.solution: If I spend a lot of money, I'll need to withdraw from my account.

examples.problem: If my alarm clock had gone off this morning, I wouldn't have overslept.
examples.solution: If I hadn't overslept, I wouldn't have missed the bus.

sections.title: Lesson 6: A Car Loan
sections.definition: Vocabulary related to car loans and financing.
sections.explanation: Down payment: initial payment. Monthly instalments: regular payments. Investment: money put into something expecting profit. Car insurance: protection against accidents.
sections.notes: Used cars often have lower monthly payments.

examples.problem: down payment =
examples.solution: initial payment made when buying something on credit

examples.problem: monthly instalments =
examples.solution: regular monthly payments to repay a loan

examples.problem: Nour got a car loan, so he could buy a car.
examples.solution: If Nour hadn't got a car loan, he wouldn't have been able to buy a car.

sections.title: Letter Completion - Car Loan
sections.definition: Completing a letter with banking vocabulary.
sections.explanation: Loan, banker, owner, down payment, instalments, insurance are key terms.
sections.notes: A car loan can be paid back over several years with monthly instalments.

examples.problem: I get a good _______
It wasn't too hard to get a car _______
My _______ is very nice.

sections.title: Lesson 7: Formal and Informal Letters
sections.definition: Differences between formal and informal letter writing.
sections.explanation: Formal: to people you don't know well, no contractions, standard expressions. Informal: to friends and family, contractions, friendly language.
rules: Formal: Dear Mr/Ms, Yours sincerely/faithfully, no contractions
Informal: Dear [first name], Best wishes, contractions are fine
sections.notes: Passive forms are common in formal letters.

examples.problem: Dear Mr Smith,
Dear Rashid,
This letter is to inform you ...
Thanks for your letter.

sections.title: Letter of Complaint
sections.definition: Writing a formal letter to complain about a banking issue.
sections.explanation: Include: reason for writing, problem description, request for action, polite closing.
sections.notes: Use standard formal expressions like 'I am writing to inform you', 'Could you please', 'Yours sincerely'.

examples.problem: What problem did Samira have?
examples.solution: Her name was misspelt on her cheque book (Sanira instead of Samira).

examples.problem: What does she ask her banker to do?
examples.solution: Send a new cheque book with her name correctly spelt and information about online banking.

sections.title: Lesson 8: I apologize for any misunderstanding
sections.definition: Writing formal emails and letters to enquire about banking services.
sections.explanation: Use polite expressions to ask about ATM cards, telephone banking, and account services.
sections.notes: Formal letters often use 'I am writing to enquire', 'Could you please', 'I was told that'.

examples.problem: Could you please let me know
examples.solution: when I will receive my card?

examples.problem: I was told that
examples.solution: I would receive it within a week.

examples.problem: If I had an ATM card,
examples.solution: I could withdraw money on my way to work.

sections.title: Writing: Letter of Complaint
sections.definition: Writing a formal complaint letter to the bank.
sections.explanation: Choose a problem like lack of ATM machines or inconvenient opening hours. Explain the issue clearly and request action.
sections.notes: Write 100-120 words in a formal style.

sections.title: Lesson 9: Making Money
sections.definition: Understanding savings, investments, and financial planning.
sections.explanation: Topics include: starting to save early, different types of accounts, investing in stocks/shares, property investment, financial wisdom.
sections.notes: Compound interest helps savings grow over time. Investments carry risk.

examples.problem: When we are children,
examples.solution: we are taught the value of saving.

examples.problem: We can get more interest
examples.solution: if we keep our money in our account for a long time.

examples.problem: What are we told to do when we start work?
examples.solution: To put some money aside for the future.

sections.title: Vocabulary: Financial Terms
sections.definition: Key vocabulary for banking and finance.
sections.explanation: Current account, withdraw, minimum balance, value, loan, statement, PIN, instalments are essential terms.
sections.notes: Interest can be paid on savings and charged on loans.

examples.problem: A _______ account comes with a debit card.
You can _______ money from an ATM machine.
The _______ of an investment can go down as well as up.

sections.title: Unit 6 Test - Vocabulary
sections.definition: Testing banking vocabulary.
sections.explanation: Verbs: open, deposit, charge, maintain, make, pay. Nouns: statement, withdrawal, instalments, down payment, branch.
sections.notes: Understanding collocations like 'open an account', 'make a withdrawal', 'pay interest'.

examples.problem: My father _______ an account for me.
I _______ 5 million Iraqi dinars this morning.
Banks usually _______ a fee if you are overdrawn.

sections.title: Unit 6 Test - Conditionals
sections.definition: Testing conditional sentences.
sections.explanation: Choose correct conditional forms based on context.
sections.notes: Pay attention to time references and whether the situation is real or hypothetical.

examples.problem: If I open a student account, I _______ maintain a minimum balance.
If I had known it was raining, I _______ brought an umbrella.
If I _______ a computer, we could search the internet.

sections.title: Unit 6 Test - Passive Voice
sections.definition: Testing passive voice formation.
sections.explanation: Form passive sentences using correct tense of 'to be' + past participle.
sections.notes: Questions in passive: be + subject + past participle.

examples.problem: When was the mail delivered?
The windows are being replaced next week.
examples.solution: The windows are being replaced next week.

examples.problem: These books were left in the classroom.
examples.solution: These books were left in the classroom.

sections.title: Unit 6 Test - Deductions and Writing
sections.definition: Testing present/past deductions and formal letter writing.
sections.explanation: Use must/may/might/can't with infinitive or have + past participle. Write a formal complaint letter about an unauthorized withdrawal.
sections.notes: Letter should be 100-120 words, formal style, explaining the problem and requesting action.

examples.problem: Hazem _______ making a lot of money; he's just bought a new car!
Rasha didn't make that payment. She _______
